coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric sensing of using N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine/triiodide ions conjugate in human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Servarayan, Karthika Lakshmi
Sundaram, Ellairaja
Sivasamy, Vasantha Vairathevar
coredata.dc:description: As an important neurotransmitter, dopamine has been playing a vital role in central nervous system and its variation of levels has leads to plenty of neurological and psychiatric disorders. Among good number of colorimetric dopamine biosensors, our group has designed more sensitive colorimetric sensor and reported as first organic chromophore based sensor for dopamine. The developed sensor is a combination of simple conjugate namely N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine (PMBD)/triiodide ions. Under optimized conditions, the sensor has shown wide range of detection towards dopamine from 5 to 475 pM range and the LOD was calculated as 0.02 pM. Interestingly, the sensing range of our sensor falls in pM level, it is more useful in clinical applications because the dopamine concentration may varies from pM to nM level in human serum at different parts of the human body. The real time analysis of the developed sensor has been tested in human serum samples collected from different persons. This physiological detection ranges captivates by the clinical technicians for the diagnosis of dopamine in serum for various brain related issues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric sensor
Chromophore
Triiodide ions
Neurotransmitter
Dopamine
Human Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Greener protocol for synthesis of N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine
Development of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate sensor
Analysis of DA in human serum by direct and standard addition methods
UV–vis spectral analysis of PMBD and PMBD/Triiodide conjugate
NMR titration study between PMBD and triiodide ions
Selective recognition of DA using PMBD/triiodide conjugate
Competitive performances of PMBD/Triiodide ions conjugate towards DA
Quantification of DA using PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate
Finding of stoichiometry of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate using job’s continuation method
Density Functional Theory (DFT) studies for PMBD, PMBD/Triiodide and PMBD/DA
Qualitative analysis of DA using UV lamp
Real time analysis of DA in human serum by direct and standard addition methods

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Science and Technology
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by RUSA Phase II Scheme, New Delhi, India (Grant No.: 007-R2/RUSA/MKU/2020-2021). Hence, the author KLS is registered her thanks to RUSA for offering the fellowship for pursuing her Ph.D. program. The author also thanks DST-PURSE, FIST, IRHPA School of Chemistry, and Central Instrumentation Facility, Madurai Kamaraj University for offering NMR and Spectrofluorimeter.

Prof. Vasantha Vairathevar Sivasamy , Ph.D. in Electrochemistry and has more than 25 years of research experience and more than 13 years of teaching experience. She has been doing research in electrochemical and optical biosensors, Electroplating, Corrosions, Fuel cells and Natural Products. She was a postdoctoral fellow at National Taipei University of Technology, and National Taiwan University and recipient of the Brain-Pool Scientist Fellowship from Korea Institute of Science & Technology, South Korea. She has published more than 90 articles, 3 book chapters, and 1 patent. Her article published in Electroanalytical Chemistry received the best citation award in 2009. Currently, she is a Registrar at the Madurai Kamaraj University and course coordinator for PG Diploma in Industrial Metal Finishing.

This work is financially supported by RUSA Phase II Scheme, New Delhi, India (Grant No.: 007-R2/RUSA/MKU/2020-2021). Hence, the author KLS is registered her thanks to RUSA for offering the fellowship for pursuing her Ph.D. program. The author also thanks DST-PURSE, FIST, IRHPA School of Chemistry, and Central Instrumentation Facility, Madurai Kamaraj University for offering NMR and Spectrofluorimeter.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-Vis spectra of PMBD probe (A), optimization of triiodide concentrations based on the UV responses of PMBD (B) [Concentration of PMBD is 0.5 µM in DMSO and stock triiodide concentration is 6 × 10−4 M].

Selectivity studies based on the absorbance changes in presence of interfering biomolecules and metal ions at higher concentrations (A) and their corresponding bar diagram (B), statistical data (C) [Concentration of PMBD is 0.5 µM, stock triiodide concentration is 6 × 10−4 M, interfering biomolecules and metal cations are 0.001 M] [A-PMBD/Triiodide, B-DA, C- AA, D-CR, E-CRTN, F- GLU, G-U, H-UA, I-Ca2+, J-EP, K-NE, L-L-TY, M- K+, N- Na+, O- Fe2+, P-Zn2+].[AA- Ascorbic Acid, CR- Creatine, CRTN- Creatinine, GLU-Glucose, U- Urea, UA- Uric Acid, EP- epinephrine, NE- norepinephrine, L-TY – L-Tyrosine].

Quenching in absorbance of the PMBD at 293 nm while varying the DA concentration from 5 to 475 pM (A), and corresponding linear plot (B). [Concentration of PMBD probe is 0.5 µM, stock triiodide concentration is 6 × 10−4 M and stock DA concentration is 1000 pM].
Optimized geometry of PMBD, PMBD/Triiodide and PMBD/Triiodide/DA.
DFT analysis of PMBD, PMBD/Triiodide and PMBD/DA.

Detection of DA in collected human serum samples directly (A-C) from three different persons (sample code 1–3) and also in DA spiked serum samples (D-F). [Concentration of PMBD is 0.5 µM; Triiodide ions are 3.6 µM; Spiked DA is 50 & 100 pM, and optimized volume of processed serum samples are 20 µL].
Schematic representation for the developed colorimetric DA sensor.
Comparative table for the performances of the DA sensor with earlier reports.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric sensing of using N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine/triiodide ions conjugate in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Natural Products Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625 021, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Natural Products Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Natural Products Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai – 625 021, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vivekananda College, Tiruvedakam West, Madurai 625 234, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Vivekananda College, Tiruvedakam West

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Vivekananda College, Tiruvedakam West, Madurai – 625 234, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important neurotransmitter, dopamine has been playing a vital role in central nervous system and its variation of levels has leads to plenty of neurological and psychiatric disorders. Among good number of colorimetric dopamine biosensors, our group has designed more sensitive colorimetric sensor and reported as first organic chromophore based sensor for dopamine. The developed sensor is a combination of simple conjugate namely N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine (PMBD)/triiodide ions. Under optimized conditions, the sensor has shown wide range of detection towards dopamine from 5 to 475 pM range and the LOD was calculated as 0.02 pM. Interestingly, the sensing range of our sensor falls in pM level, it is more useful in clinical applications because the dopamine concentration may varies from pM to nM level in human serum at different parts of the human body. The real time analysis of the developed sensor has been tested in human serum samples collected from different persons. This physiological detection ranges captivates by the clinical technicians for the diagnosis of dopamine in serum for various brain related issues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In our central nervous system, dopamine (3,4-dihydroxyphenethylamine, DA), is one of the important abundant catecholamine, and biogenic amine based neurotransmitter which plays several important roles like control the activities like movement, cognition, emotion, memory and creativity and also control the activities of hormonal, renal, metabolic and cardiovascular systems [1–6]. It is present in nanomoles in plasma and picomoles in pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma cells. The normal range of DA in our human blood serum is 1–30 pg/mL (195.8 pmol/L) and hence it is very difficult to determine the DA in clinical application with existing analytical techniques [7–9]. In addition, the unambiguous and tactful determination of DA is vitally important in the diagnostics of various diseases such as neurological and psychiatric disorders like schizophrenia, oarkinson's disease, alzheimer’s disease, bipolar disorder, huntington's disease, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), tourette's syndrome, pleasurable feelings and euphoria [10–13]. Also, the disparity of DA levels has plays a vital role in schizophrenia disease, depressive disorder, somnipathy, addictive behaviors, such as shopaholism and drug abuse [14]. All these serious clinical issues have clearly reveals the necessity for the detection of DA in human serum. For detection of DA, there are plenty of analytical techniques are available such as fluorescence spectrometry [15], high performance liquid chromatography [16], capillary electrophoresis [17], liquid chromatography-electrospray tandem mass spectrometry [18], electrochemical techniques [19,20], chemiluminescence spectroscopy [21]. However, the aforementioned method is involving remarkable risks like complicated pre-concentration, sample pretreatment time-consuming steps, and requires sophisticated equipment which limits their wide applicability. In recent sensing era, a good number of sensing platforms, including electrochemical [22–24], fluorescent [25] and colorimetric sensors [26–28] have been developed for the determination of DA. In case of electrochemical sensing assay, one of the foremost problems have been encountered with the DA is the interference of coexisting electro active species, such as ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and their structural analogs like epinephrine, norepineprine and tyrosine especially in biological fluids. These species always coexist with DA in biological fluids and the oxidation potentials of all three being adjacent [29] and also, electrochemical methods are usually require sophisticated modification of the electrode. In recent years, optical based sensing platform had been introduced by various groups and they have demonstrated the good sensitivity and selectivity towards DA by using some synthetic fluorescent quantum dots and nanoparticles. Using MoS2nano- hybridization, FRET quenching and inner filter based sensor has been reported for DA from 2.5 nM to 5.0 µM and the detection limit is 0.9 nM [30]. By utilizing the host-guest chemistry of mono-6-amino-β-cyclodextrin (NH2-β-CD)/gold nanoclusters, another fluorescent sensor was reported in the linear range of 5–1000 nM with the detection limit of 2 nM [31]. Recently, an azamonardine product based DA sensor has been published based on the reaction between resorcinol and DA that allows the detection of DA with linear range of 10 nM - 20 µM and the limit of detection is estimated to be 1.8 nM [32]. Very recently, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) based fluorescence sensor has been reported with good linear range of 1–200 µM with the LOD of 0.07 µM [33]. Polydopamine nanoparticles based DA sensor has also been reported with the linear range from 1 to 200 µM and the detection limit was found as 0.3 μM [34]. By using ficin-H2O2-tyramine system, new type of sensor has been designed and applied for the detection of DA from 0.05 to 12.0 μM with the LOD of 46 nM [35]. Some colorimetric biosensors have also been developed to minimize the potential interference and thus improve the accuracy for the detection of DA. Ai et al., had reported a colorimetric sensor for DA, based on the interaction between DA and AuNPs solutions, hydroxyl groups of the DA adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs. After the addition of Cu2+ ions to AuNPs solution containing DA, results in the formation of aggregation of AuNPs due to the interaction between Cu2+ ions and DA. This sensor has shown the linear range from 5 × 10−7 M to 1 × 10−5 M and the detection limit was found to be 2 × 10−7 M [36]. Recently, β-CD modified AuNPs based sensor was determined the DA concentration and the detection limit was calculated as 20 nM [37]. Another plasmonic colorimetric DA sensor has been reported using AuNPs the linear range found from 10 to 125 mg L−1 with the LOD of 12.85 ± 1.38 mg L−1 [38]. TSIL functionalized hexagonal silver NPs (TSIL-Ag NPs) based DA sensor has been designed for DA from 0.1 to 7.5 µM and the LOD was 0.031 µM [39]. NiCo2S4-rGO-TMB based system has also been reported as a colorimetric sensor for DA from 0.1 to 7.5 µM and achieved 0.42 μM of LOD [40]. AuNPs/ Nylon-6 polymer nano fiber flexible sensor has been designed and applied for the detection of DA level of 5 × 10−7 M [41]. Recently, microfluidic paper-based analytical device has been reported for the detection of DA based on the color change reactions between ferric chloride and DA. Here, DA reacts with ferric ion, it reduced to Fe2+ that afterward it reacts with phenanthroline to form the red color and the linear range was found from 0.527 to 4.75 μmol/L with the LOD of 0.37 μmol/L [42]. All these sensors discussed above are mostly utilizing nanomaterials like gold, silver, and quantum dots as sensing platforms which are highly expensive, more time-consuming, fluorescent decay due to aggregation, lack of selectivity and sensitivity and limited absorbance ranges towards DA. Hence, a highly selective and sensitive, fast response, a simple and cost-effective synthetic protocol is required for the detection of DA. Herein, our group has synthesized a simple Schiff base ligand namely N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine along with Triiodide ions as a chromophore which was synthesized via one-pot condensation reaction and applied for the detection of Dain serum samples [43]. This synthetic methodology is very simple, less time consuming and less side products. Already many reports are available for the detection of metal ions and some biomolecules using Schiff base fluorescent molecules [44], but, this is the first report for the detection of DA using organic Schiff base as a chromophore [45]. The developed sensor has shown good range of detection with pM detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials, methods and experimental techniques
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde, o-phenylenediamine, HPLC grade ethanol, acetic acid, dimethyl sulfoxide, ascorbic acid, creatine, creatinine, L-dopamine, D-glucose, uric acid, urea, chloride salts of Ca2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+ and nitrate salts of Zn2+, K+ and Na+ were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as such. The human serum was collected from the volunteers of School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University and with the help of trained research scholars from Department of Immunology, School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai-21, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods
Greener protocol for synthesis of N′-(pyrene-1-ylmethylene) benzene-1,2-diamine
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.5 g of pyrene 1- carboxaldehyde was completely dissolved in 15 mL of absolute ethanol. To this solution, about 0.4 g of o-phenylenediamine was added slowly and then stirred for 5 min at room temperature. Secondly, the mixture solution was kept under ultra-wave sonicator (bath type) of 1.5 Hz frequency for 30 min and then it was allowed to stand at room temperature and finally it was poured into ice cold water. A yellow colored powder PMBD (Scheme S1) was obtained. The product was filtered, and washed with ethanol and diethyl ether and dried over MgSO4 vacuum (2.5 g, 99 %). The solid product PMBD was obtained as a single compound which was confirmed through TLC using the mixture of PET ether and diethyl ether [4.5:0.5 v/v]. Obtained crude product was purified by simple re-crystallization method by using hot ethanol and then used for characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PMBD
ce:section.ce:para: The purified product structure was confirmed with NMR, ESI-MS and FT-IR spectroscopy. The corresponding spectral values are given below and corresponding data was given in supporting information Fig. S1a–d .
ESI-MS.
C23H16N2 (calculated mass- 318.11): Observed mass = 319.12, Yield: 98.5 %.
FT-IR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To 2 mL of PMBD (0.5 µM) in DMSO, 200 µL of slight acidic solution of triiodide ions (0.01 M) were added and then shaken thoroughly for complete solubility and then corresponding absorbance was noted. To this mixture, 5 pM of DA was added, shaken and kept for 3 min at room temperature and the corresponding absorbance was noted. The same procedure was repeated for other DA concentrations. And a linear plot was drawn between the corresponding absorbance changes of PMBD Vs concentration of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DA in human serum by direct and standard addition methods
ce:section.ce:para: All the blood samples were collected with the help of certified person at MK University. For this study, 5 mL of human blood serum (each 5 mL) was collected from the healthy volunteers. The serum was isolated by means of centrifugation process at 60,000 rpm for 20 min time interval and then kept at 0°C for 2 hr and it was further maintained at −4°C before analyzing. Initially, to 2 mL of PMBD/triiodide conjugates (in DMSO), 20 µL of serum samples were added and the absorbance changes were observed. The amount of unknown DA present in the serum samples was determined from the linear plot.

In standard addition method, to 1 mL of serum sample, 50 and 100 pM of DA was spiked. Then it was further treated with 2 mL of 0.5 µM PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate in DMSO. And then it was kept for incubation nearly 2–5 min. After the incubation, the absorbance changes were recorded under optimized conditions. The total spiked DA concentration in serum samples was calculated based on the linear plot and the corresponding values are tabulated. From these values the recovery percentage was calculated and given in Table 2. In clinical point of view, both these methods are clearly explaining the applicability of developed sensor for the quantification of DA in human blood samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Brucker 300 MHz instrument were used for NMR characterization at Madurai Kamaraj University. Chemical shifts were reported in ppm at 25 °C using DMSO as solvent containing a trace quantity of tetramethylsilane (TMS) as internal standard. FT-IR spectra was measured using Shimadzu. UV-Visible spectra were recorded by using the double beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer (JASCO-V-730) and ESI-MS data were recorded using Jeol GCMS GC-Mate II. Density functional theory calculations of molecular orbital surfaces carried out using B3LYP theory with 6–31 G(d,p) basis set and EUTECH pH meter was used to measure the pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussions
UV–vis spectral analysis of PMBD and PMBD/Triiodide conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, UV-Vis responses of the chromophore, PMBD was analysed in DMSO solvent and there were two major absorption peaks were observed at 293 & 364 nm which may be attributed due to the occurrence of π-π * and n-π * transitions, respectively ( Fig. 1A). We have also optimized the effect of time at which the maximum absorbance is occurred. The absorbance of PMBD was noted by keeping the solution for different time interval from 0 to 60 min and interestingly probe PMBD has exhibited a maximum absorbance within 2 min and after that there were no remarkable change in absorbance and hence the optimized time for maximum absorbance of PMBD is 2 mins (Fig S2 A and B). We have also crosschecked the stability of probe by recording the absorbance for a month and the obtained results clearly reveals that PMBD is showing good photo stability even for a month (Fig S2 C and D). Colorimetric sensor platform was developed by conjugating the triiodide ions with PMBD chromophore. Initially, we have done the pH studies from 2 to 13 for PMBD triiodide ions conjugate. As our conjugate doesn’t have any acid or base labile group, we didn’t observe any remarkable changes in absorbance (Fig. S3 a). Then, different concentration of triiodide ions from 0.6 µM to 3.6 µM was added into the 0.5 µM of PMBD solution, and an increment in absorbance intensity of the two peaks was noted (Fig. 1B) which might be due to the substitution of I- ion with imine nitrogen bond and finally HI was removed and this was supported with NMR titrations (Fig. S3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR titration study between PMBD and triiodide ions
ce:section.ce:para: The binding mode between the PMBD and triiodide conjugate was confirmed by performing the NMR titrations. The PMBD has shown a sharp imine H-peak at 12 ppm (Fig. S1a) while varying the concentration of triiodide ions, the corresponding intensity of imine H-peak decreased and finally disappeared (Fig. S3). Based on these 1H NMR titration study, the binding of iodine ions with imine was confirmed and it was happened through substitution reactions. The imine hydrogen present in PMBD may be replaced as hydrogen iodide molecule and form a new nitrogen-iodine bond in PMBD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective recognition of DA using PMBD/triiodide conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: Selective recognition of a particular biomolecules present in human biofluids like serum, urine and saliva has been playing a vital role in DA based sensors. The developed sensor platform PMBD-triiodide ions conjugate has shown a selective response towards DA alone in presence of other interferences like ascorbic acid, creatinine, creatine, uric acid, urea, glucose and biologically relevant metal ions like Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+ and Fe2+. Remarkably, we have also done the selectivity studies with structurally relevant biomolecules catecholamine like epinephrine, norepinephrine and L-tyrosine. In current study, PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate was used as a colorimetric sensing platform in order to analyze the selective recognition of DA. Under optimized conditions, various solution of above mentioned interferences were added individually to the solution of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate and the corresponding absorbance at respective wavelengths was observed. For DA alone a drastic decrease in absorption intensity was noted and for the rest of the interferences, negligible intensity change was noted. Specifically, epinephrine, norepinephrine and L-tyrosine biomolecules have shown only negligible decrements in absorbance and they didn’t quench the absorbance of PMBD like dopamine. Our developed sensor is chemical reaction based chemodosimeter type sensor involving free primary –NH2 group of DA with iodide substituted PMBD and these three analogues are showing some variation in their structure. In epinephrine we have only secondary methyl substituted amine and also a –OH group. Hence there may be a steric hindrance of methyl & -OH group which results the inhibition of nucleophilic addition reaction based mechanism, Whereas, in tyrosine, the primary amine in resonance with nearby carboxyl group and hence the effective reaction is not possible ( Fig. 2). From these observations, it is clearly shown that the developed sensor platform is highly selective towards DA in presence of other interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive performances of PMBD/Triiodide ions conjugate towards DA
ce:section.ce:para: Competitive detection also assists to confirm the selective nature of the sensing platform in presence of DA along with interfering biomolecules and metal ions in the same solution. Competitive performances were done using 2 mL of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate (0.5 µM) with DA (100 µL, 500 pM), in addition with interfering metal ions and biomolecules (200 µL, 500 pM). All the interferences were added one by one in the same solution and the corresponding absorbance changes were observed at 293 & 364 nm. And there were minimal change in absorbance was noted for all the interferences. Based on competitive titrations, the selective binding of DA with PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate is again proved (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of DA using PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The developed PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate was used as a very good colorimetric assay to quantify the DA under optimized conditions. Different concentrations of DA were added initially from 5 to 510 pM into the solution of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate and the corresponding absorption changes were recorded. There was a gradual decrement in absorbance intensity was observed after each addition of DA to PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate solution ( Fig. 3A). Using standard formula, the limit of detection of the sensor is calculated as 0.02 pM and the linear range was found as 5 – 475 pM (Fig. 3B). When compare to the previous reports, our sensor has shown a wider detection range for DA ( Table 1). The stoichiometry of the bioassay is found to be 1:1 and the quenching efficiency are calculated as 88.5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Finding of stoichiometry of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate using job’s continuation method
ce:section.ce:para: Using the Job’s continuation method, it is possible to determine the stoichiometry of any chromophore with targeted analyte. For this particular study, both the PMBD/triiodide conjugate and DA were fixed at same concentrations. The absorbance changes of chromophore PMBD was firstly observed. Followed by, molefraction of DA the absorbance changes for the various mole fraction of DA from 0.1 to 0.9 with PMBD/triiodide conjugate were determined. A plot was drawn between the molefraction of DA and corresponding absorbance differences of PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate with and without DA. By calculating the intercept point (∼0.5 mol fraction) of the plot (Fig. S5), the stoichiometry of developed sensor was calculated as 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quenching efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching efficiency of any TURN-OFF optical biosensors could be defined as (A0 - A)/A0, where A0 and A represent the absorbance intensity of PMBD chromophore in the absence (A0) and presence (A) of DA. The linear plot is between A0/A and concentration of DA is shown in Fig. S6 . It is clear that the plot indicates a good linear relationship in a wide concentration range and the quenching efficiency was calculated as 88.5 % under optimized sensor conditions. We have also analyzed the repeatability of our developed sensor for three successive trials and the results have clearly support that accuracy nature of our designed sensor towards DA (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density Functional Theory (DFT) studies for PMBD, PMBD/Triiodide and PMBD/DA
ce:section.ce:para: DFT studies were carried out to support the mechanism of interaction between PMBD and triiodide and PMBD/triiodide and DA. The following basic sets B3LYP16–31 G (d, p) LANL2DZECP and B3LYP16–31 G and 6–311 G were used for DFT calculations by using Gaussian 09 programme. The corresponding optimized geometry ( Fig. 4) and molecular energy level diagram was shown in ( Fig. 5).

From these data, they clearly reveal that in PMBD molecule, the initial electron density is localized slightly higher in pyrene part compare to cyclic imine part in HOMO. In LUMO, there is charge polarization from o-phenylene to pyrene part with the energy difference of 3.46 eV. When triiodide is added, the in HOMO the electron density is localized is higher than that of PMBD before the addition of triiodide ions. This may be due to the formation PMBD/triiodide conjugate. In LUMO state of the conjugate, the electron density completely localized on –N-I bond with energy difference of 2.60 eV which is mainly due to the heavy atom effect of iodine [79]. After the addition of DA molecule with solution containing PMBD/Triiodide conjugate, the electron density distribution attains the original state of charge distribution of PMBD in HOMO state. This phenomenon shows that the absence of heavy atom effect of iodine. However, in LUMO state there is a slight charge polarization towards 0-phenylene part to pyrene part with energy difference of 3.59 eV. Initially, the absorbance was increased after binding with iodine which may attributes to occurrence of wide electronic charge transfer between the cyclic imine to the iodine part. After binding with DA the iodine ion may be removed and electronic charge was primarily localized on pyrene unit and there is a minimal charge transfer between cyclic imine parts to the pyrene moiety as like in PMBD and hence the quenching in absorbance was observed. From these energy level DFT calculations (Fig. 5), it was clearly evinced that the developed sensor has adopted the charge transfer mechanism based on proposed energy level diagram ( Scheme 1). The plausible mechanism for the developed sensor was given in supporting information (Scheme S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative analysis of DA using UV lamp
ce:section.ce:para: The designed sensor has shown a remarkable color changes towards DA in presence of other interferents. Under optimized sensor conditions, we have initially observed the color changes of PMBD and it was colorless in DMSO solution. And then triiodide ions were added to the PMBD solution and the color was changed into slight yellow in color. Now DA and other interferents were added individually to this PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate. Interestingly DA alone has shown an intense pink color and rest of the interferents did not show any remarkable color changes (Fig. S8). Hence this developed sensing assay can be used as good colorimetric tool for the qualitative detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real time analysis of DA in human serum by direct and standard addition methods
ce:section.ce:para: Prepartion of blood serum was detaily given in experimental section. Centrifuged human blood serum was used as such for the direct detection of DA ( Fig. 6A-C). An absorbance changes of the developed sensor PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate was tested for above serum samples and recorded. In order to further support accuary of the developed sensor, unknown serum was further crosschecked by standard addition method (Fig. 6D-F). By spiking known concentartions of DA to the crude blood samples. The same quenching behaviour was abserved during spiking of different concentartions of DA. Standard linear graph (Fig. 3B) was used in order to quantify the of DA present in these samples and the corresponding values are given in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, we designed a synthetic chromophore PMBD/triiodide ions conjugate based optical colorimetric sensor for DA detection in human serum samples. Chromophore PMBD was synthesized via greener route and their photophysical properties were studied initially based on absorbance changes. Under optimized sensing conditions, developed sensor has selectively detected DA for a wide range of concentrations from 5 to 475 pM and LOD is found as 0.02 pM. They also has been exhibited a remarkable color changes in presence of DA and hence acted as a good colorimetric assay for DA. Designed sensing assay has been applied for real time monitoring of DA in human serum samples, and discriminates all the possible interfering molecules present in the human bio-fluids. These results have shown a good agreement with our optimized linear responses and so it could be a better and simple route for analysis of DA compare to existing tools in central nervous system based neurological disorders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Ms. Karthika Lakshmi Servarayan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis and Editing. Dr. Ellairaja Sundaram: Writing- Original draft, Resources and Formal analysis. Prof. Vasantha Vairathevar Sivasamy: Supervision, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by RUSA Phase II Scheme, New Delhi, India (Grant No.: 007-R2/RUSA/MKU/2020-2021). Hence, the author KLS is registered her thanks to RUSA for offering the fellowship for pursuing her Ph.D. program. The author also thanks DST-PURSE, FIST, IRHPA School of Chemistry, and Central Instrumentation Facility, Madurai Kamaraj University for offering NMR and Spectrofluorimeter.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supporting information
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134202.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary material
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the detection of neurotransmitters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Si, E. Song, Recent advances in the detection of neurotransmitters. Chemosensors. 6 (2018) 1.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.G. Beyene, I.R. McFarlane, R.L. Pinals, M.P. Landry, Stochastic simulation of dopamine neuromodulation for implementation of fluorescent neurochemical probes in the striatal extracellular space. ACS Chem. Neurosci. 8 (2017) 2275–2289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-signal luminescent detection of dopamine by a single type of lanthanide-doped nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ling, R. Shi, J. Zhang, D. Liu, M. Weng, C. Zhang, W. Huang, Dual-signal luminescent detection of dopamine by a single type of lanthanide-doped nanoparticles. ACS Sens. 3 (2018) 1683–1689.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.R. Suhito, N. Angeline, T.H. Kim, Nanomaterial-modified hybrid platforms for precise electrochemical detection of dopamine. BioChip J., 13 (2019) 20–29.

J. Feher, Cells, Synapses, and Neurotransmitters, Quant. Hum. Physiol., 1 (2012) 307–320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel dopamine electrochemical sensor based on La3+/ZnO nanoflower modified graphite screen printed electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tajik, H. Beitollahi, M.R. Aflatoonian, A novel dopamine electrochemical sensor based on La3+/ZnO nanoflower modified graphite screen printed electrode. J. Electrochem. Sci. Eng. 9 (2019) 187–195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dawson-type vanadium-substituted tungstophosphate-modified ITO electrode: preparation, characterization and electrochemical determination of dopamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, Y. Zhao, D. Zhang, S. Rong, H. Ma, Dawson-Type Vanadium-Substituted Tungstophosphate-Modified ITO Electrode: Preparation, Characterization and Electrochemical Determination of Dopamine. Int. J. Electrochem. Sci. 14 (2019) 3595–3609.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective and sensitive detection of dopamine in the presence of ascorbic acid and uric acid at a Sonogel-Carbon L-Histidine modified electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.B. Atyah, L. Bounab, M. Choukairi, R. Khamlichi, D. Bouchta, F. Chaouket, I. Raissouni, Selective and sensitive detection of dopamine in the presence of ascorbic acid and uric acid at a Sonogel-Carbon l-Histidine modified electrode. J. Mater. Environ. Sci. 9 (2018) 66–76.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent gold nanoclusters for selective detection of dopamine in cerebrospinal fluid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Govindaraju, S.R. Ankireddy, B. Viswanath, J. Kim, K. Yun, Fluorescent gold nanoclusters for selective detection of dopamine in cerebrospinal fluid. Sci. rep. 7 (2017) 1–12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive detection of dopamine at ionic liquid functionalized RGO/ZIF-8 nanocomposite-modified electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Cheng, Y. Fan, X. Shen, H. Liang, H. Highly sensitive detection of dopamine at ionic liquid functionalized RGO/ZIF-8 nanocomposite-modified electrode. J. Nanomater. (2019) 1–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A core-shell molybdenum nanoparticles entrapped f-MWCNT s hybrid nanostructured material based non-enzymatic biosensor for electrochemical detection of dopamine neurotransmitter in biological samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Keerthi, G. Boopathy, S.M. Chen, T.W. Chen, B.S. Lou, A core-shell molybdenum nanoparticles entrapped f-MWCNT s hybrid nanostructured material based non-enzymatic biosensor for electrochemical detection of dopamine neurotransmitter in biological samples. Sci. Rep. 9 (2019) 1–12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine binding and analysis in undiluted human serum and blood by the RNA-aptamer electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Alvarez-Martos, A. Moller, E.E. Ferapontova, Dopamine binding and analysis in undiluted human serum and blood by the RNA-aptamer electrode. ACS chem. Neurosci. 10 (2019) 1706–1715.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scalable nanostructured carbon electrode arrays for enhanced dopamine detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Demuru, L. Nela, N. Marchack, S.J. Holmes, D.B. Farmer, G.S. Tulevski, H. Deligianni, Scalable nanostructured carbon electrode arrays for enhanced dopamine detection. ACS sens. 3 (2018) 799–805.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of Au nanoparticle-decorated mos2 nanoslices as efficient electrocatalysts for electrochemical detection of dopamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chen, H. Chen, R. Hong, Fabrication of Au Nanoparticle-Decorated MoS2 Nanoslices as Efficient Electrocatalysts for Electrochemical Detection of Dopamine. Catalysts 9 (2019) 653.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iodide as a fluorescence quencher and promoters mechanisms and possible implications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Chmyrov, T. Sande´n, J. Widengren, Iodide as a Fluorescence Quencher and Promoters Mechanisms and Possible Implications. J. Phys. Chem. B. 114 (2010) 11282–11291.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms. Karthika Lakshmi Servarayan obtained her B.Sc., Chemistry from Lady Doak College in 2014, M.Sc., Chemistry from HKRH College in 2016, and M.Phil., from Madurai Kamaraj University, India in 2017. Currently, she is doing her Ph.D. under Prof. V. S. Vasantha at the Department of Natural Products Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India from 2019. Her main area of interest is optical biosensor.

Dr. Ellairaja Sundaram is currently working as an Assistant Professor cum Researcher at Department of Chemistry, Vivekananda College, Tiruvedakam West, Madurai- 625 234, Tamil Nadu, India. He completed his B.Sc., Chemistry at The Madura College (Autonomous), and M.Sc and Ph.D at School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai. His area of research interest is to design optical Biosensors/Immunosensors for various clinical relevant biomolecules and pathogens using synthetic organic fluorophore and Nanoparticle conjugates.

Prof. Vasantha Vairathevar Sivasamy, Ph.D. in Electrochemistry and has more than 25 years of research experience and more than 13 years of teaching experience. She has been doing research in electrochemical and optical biosensors, Electroplating, Corrosions, Fuel cells and Natural Products. She was a postdoctoral fellow at National Taipei University of Technology, and National Taiwan University and recipient of the Brain-Pool Scientist Fellowship from Korea Institute of Science & Technology, South Korea. She has published more than 90 articles, 3 book chapters, and 1 patent. Her article published in Electroanalytical Chemistry received the best citation award in 2009. Currently, she is a Registrar at the Madurai Kamaraj University and course coordinator for PG Diploma in Industrial Metal Finishing.
